title: The Verge criticises Suno’s copyright-infringement guardrails - Music Ally
text:
AI-music firm Suno may have settled the lawsuit filed against it by Warner Music Group, but it remains locked in litigation with the other two majors.
Their lawyers will be eagerly reading
The Verge’s latest investigation
, which claims that the guardrails in the Suno Studio software designed to prevent copyright-infringing outputs can easily be swerved.
This revolves around a feature for uploading tracks to edit or cover, which are supposed to be a musician’s own original material, with copyright-detection tech used to weed out any naughtiness.
“But using a basic free tool like Audacity to slow down a track to half-speed or speed it up to twice normal will often bypass the filter, and adding a burst of white noise to the start and end seems to basically guarantee success,” reported The Verge.
“You can restore the original speed and cut the white noise in Suno Studio, and the copyrighted song becomes the seed for new AI music.”
The report claims that the guardrails against pasting in copyrighted lyrics can also be avoided, with covers of Beyoncé’s ‘Freedom’ and Black Sabbath’s ‘Paranoid’ offered as proof.
The Verge warned that this music could then easily be uploaded to streaming services. However, pending further settlements and licensing deals, its report may well be coming to a legal filing in the lawsuits sooner rather than later.
Stuart Dredge
Music Ally's Head of Insight
